title: DisjointHardInterventions
tex_block_html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of nodes from $G$. Then the CADMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting on $W_2$ is the same CADMG that arises from first node-splitting on $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}. \]

% title: DisjointHardInterventions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_11}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_11}

\begin{Lem}[Disjoint hard interventions and node-splitting hard interventions commute]\label{disjoint-hard-interventions-and-node-splitting-hard-interventions-commute}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG (def \ref{def-cdmg-types}, item~i., equivalently a CDMG of def \ref{def-cdmg} that is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and recall the typing and axioms of def \ref{def-cdmg}, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). We rely throughout on the hard-intervention construction $\doit$ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} and the node-splitting hard-intervention (SWIG) construction $\swig$ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}. Let
    \[ W_1 \subseteq J \cup V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \subseteq V \]
    be two subsets of nodes of $G$ (both automatically finite, since $J \cup V$ is finite by def \ref{def-cdmg}; this matches the project's Lean encoding of $W_1$ and $W_2$ as \texttt{Finset}s), subject to the disjointness side condition
    \[ W_1 \cap W_2 \;=\; \emptyset. \]
    All three quantifiers ``for every CADMG $G$'', ``for every $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$'', and ``for every $W_2 \subseteq V$ with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$'' are read \emph{universally}; in particular the (vacuously disjoint) case $W_1 = W_2 = \emptyset$ is admitted.

    \emph{Reading of ``CADMG'' in the LN's prose.}
    The LN's literal text opens with ``Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG'' and refers in the prose to ``the CADMG obtained from $\ldots$'' for both sides of the displayed equality. Both sides of the equality are produced by applying $\doit$ (def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}) and $\swig$ (def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}) to $G$. The constructor of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} requires the CADMG hypothesis on its input -- on $G$ for the RHS's inner $\swig(W_2)$, and on $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ for the LHS's outer $\swig(W_2)$. Acyclicity of $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ under the CADMG hypothesis on $G$ is established by \ref{claim_3_3} (\emph{AcyclicPreservedUnderDo}); acyclicity of $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ under the same hypothesis is established by \ref{claim_3_9} (\emph{SwigAcyclic}). Hence both sides of the displayed equality are CADMGs. At the underlying CDMG-type level, the asserted equality below is first an equality of CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}) -- componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ -- and the upgrade to an equality of CADMGs is automatic once both sides are known to be acyclic CDMGs (acyclicity is a propositional condition on a fixed CDMG, transported along the CDMG-equality).

    \emph{Well-typedness of the inner SWIG on the LHS.}
    The LHS $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ requires its outer $\swig(W_2)$ to act on a CADMG whose output-node set contains $W_2$, i.e.\ $W_2 \subseteq V_{\doit(W_1)}$ per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s precondition (the CADMG-ness of $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ itself comes from \ref{claim_3_3} on top of $G$ being a CADMG). By item~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} we have $V_{\doit(W_1)} = V \sm W_1$, and the inclusion
    \[ W_2 \;\subseteq\; V \sm W_1 \;=\; V_{\doit(W_1)} \]
    follows from $W_2 \subseteq V$ together with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$. Hence the LHS is defined per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}. Well-typedness of the RHS $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ is spelled out in the carrier-reading paragraph below the displayed equality.

    \emph{The conclusion.}
    The CADMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting (in the SWIG sense of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}) on $W_2$ equals the CADMG obtained from first node-splitting (in the same sense) on $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$:
    \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}. \]
    The equality is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}: equal input-node sets ($J_{\text{LHS}} = J_{\text{RHS}}$), equal output-node sets ($V_{\text{LHS}} = V_{\text{RHS}}$), equal directed-edge sets ($E_{\text{LHS}} = E_{\text{RHS}}$), and equal bidirected-edge sets ($L_{\text{LHS}} = L_{\text{RHS}}$); the conjunctive unpacking into these four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

    \medskip\emph{Carrier reading of the equality.}
    By def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} the operation $\swig(W_2)$ shifts the carrier from $J \cup V$ to the tagged-disjoint-union carrier
    \[ J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp J \dcup W_2^i \rp \;\cup\; \lp (V \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o \rp, \]
    where $W_2^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W_2 \rC$ and $W_2^i := \lC w^i \st w \in W_2 \rC$ are the two type-level tagged copies of $W_2$ introduced in def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} (so $W_2^o \cap W_2^i = \emptyset$ and both $W_2^o$ and $W_2^i$ are disjoint from every element of $J \cup V$). Crucially, the input-side tagged copy $W_2^i$ sits in $J_{\swig(W_2)}$ and the output-side tagged copy $W_2^o$ sits in $V_{\swig(W_2)}$ -- this is the distinguishing feature of $\swig$ versus the regular node-splitting $\spl$ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, whose output-node set carries \emph{both} tagged copies. By def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} the operation $\doit$ preserves the carrier $J \cup V$ of its input as a set (items~i.\ and~ii.\ give $J_{\doit(W)} \cup V_{\doit(W)} = (J \cup W) \cup (V \sm W) = J \cup V$ whenever $W \subseteq J \cup V$). Thus both sides of the displayed equality live in a CDMG over the same $\swig(W_2)$-carrier just displayed, and the asserted equality is a \emph{literal} $=$ of CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}) -- \emph{not} an equality up to a carrier-relabelling map. On the right-hand side, $\doit(W_1)$ is read as hard-intervening on the \emph{unsplit copies} of the nodes of $W_1$ inside the $\swig(W_2)$-carrier: every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in J \cup V$ (by $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$) and $w \notin W_2$ (by $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$), so $w \in J \cup (V \sm W_2)$, which is the untagged piece of $J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$ under def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s notational shorthand ``$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_2)$'' (i.e.\ $W_1$ injects into the $\swig(W_2)$-carrier as the unsplit injection $w \mapsto w$). The precondition $W_1 \subseteq J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} is thereby met by these unsplit copies, so the RHS is also defined per def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}, and the LN's $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ carrier reading agrees with the unique LN-faithful reading of $\doit(W_1)$ on it.
\end{Lem}

\end{document}

% Proof body adapted from the LN's \Claude{...} block at
% lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex lines 672-700, with the following
% refinements:
% (i) explicit citation of \ref{claim_3_3} (AcyclicPreservedUnderDo) for
%     the CADMG-hood of G_{\doit(W_1)} required by the inner \swig(W_2) on
%     the LHS (the LN's terse opening sentence "both operations are
%     well-defined in either order" glossed over this; the SWIG construction
%     of def_3_12 -- in contrast to the spl construction of def_3_11 used in
%     claim_3_8 -- requires its input to be a CADMG, so an acyclicity
%     witness must be exhibited);
% (ii) the LN's terse parenthetical "with W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset" is
%      unpacked into an explicit decomposition of ((V \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o)
%      \sm W_1 that splits the intersection across the two disjoint pieces,
%      citing the SWIG-specific tagged-construction disjointness
%      W_1 \cap W_2^o = W_1 \cap W_2^i = \emptyset at the tagged pieces and
%      the hypothesis W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset only on the untagged piece;
% (iii) the LN's "head v_2^o \in W_1 iff v_2 \in W_1" claim is unpacked
%       into two sub-cases on whether v_2 \in W_2 or v_2 \in V \sm W_2, with
%       the disjointness W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset cross-checked in the
%       v_2 \in W_2 branch;
% (iv) the bidirected-edge section carries an explicit paragraph on the
%      registered deviation `hard_intervention_l_symmetrized_removal'
%      attached to def_3_10's encoding (two-sided filter on L instead
%      of the LN's literal one-sided filter), with the reconciliation
%      under L's symmetry axiom (def_3_1) spelled out.
% The proof is organised under the LN's three section headings
% (Node sets / Directed edges / Bidirected edges); the "Node sets" section
% covers the J and V fields jointly, matching the LN's grouping and
% the four-field componentwise check of the downstream Lean proof.
\begin{proof}
We verify the equality $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ componentwise on the four fields $(J, V, E, L)$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, organised as in the LN under three section headings: ``Node sets'' (covering $J$ and $V$ jointly), ``Directed edges'' (the $E$ field), and ``Bidirected edges'' (the $L$ field).

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Well-typedness of both iteration orders.}
For the LHS $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$: the outer $\swig(W_2)$ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} requires (a) its input to be a CADMG and (b) $W_2 \subseteq V_{\doit(W_1)}$. For (a), $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ is itself a CADMG by \ref{claim_3_3} (\emph{AcyclicPreservedUnderDo}) applied to the standing CADMG hypothesis on $G$ -- this is the SWIG-specific acyclicity step that the spl analog (\refrow{claim_3_8}) did not need, since the regular node-splitting $\spl$ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting} does not require a CADMG input. For (b), by def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~ii., $V_{\doit(W_1)} = V \sm W_1$, and the inclusion $W_2 \subseteq V \sm W_1$ follows from $W_2 \subseteq V$ together with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$. For the RHS $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$: the outer $\doit(W_1)$ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} requires only $W_1 \subseteq J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$, which holds by the carrier-reading paragraph of the statement above: every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in J \cup V$ (from $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$) and $w \notin W_2$ (from $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$), so $w \in J \cup (V \sm W_2)$, the untagged piece of $J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$ under def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s unsplit shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Notational reminder.}
For $w \in W_2$ we write $w^o$, $w^i$ for the two tagged copies produced by $\swig(W_2)$ per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}; these live in the tagged-disjoint-union pieces $W_2^o$ and $W_2^i$ respectively, and are formally distinct from every element of $J \cup V$ (and from each other), per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s tagged-copies disjointness assertions $W_2^o \cap V = W_2^i \cap V = W_2^o \cap J = W_2^i \cap J = W_2^o \cap W_2^i = \emptyset$. For $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_2)$ we use def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s unsplit shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$, which leaves $v$ in the untagged piece of $J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$. \emph{Crucially for SWIG, the input copies $W_2^i$ sit in $J_{\swig(W_2)}$ and the output copies $W_2^o$ sit in $V_{\swig(W_2)}$} -- the distinguishing feature of $\swig$ versus the regular node-splitting $\spl$ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, whose output-node set carries \emph{both} tagged copies.

On the right-hand side, the outer $\doit(W_1)$ is read on the lifted intervention set ``$W_1$ inside the $\swig(W_2)$-carrier'', which by the carrier-reading paragraph of the statement is the unsplit image $\lC w \st w \in W_1 \rC \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_2)$ (using $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ and $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$); throughout the proof we write $W_1$ for this lifted set, using the unsplit shorthand $w = w^o = w^i$ for $w \in W_1$. The SWIG-specific carrier-arithmetic observation we use repeatedly below is
\[ W_1 \cap W_2^o \;=\; \emptyset \qquad\text{and}\qquad W_1 \cap W_2^i \;=\; \emptyset, \]
both of which follow from $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ together with the tagged-construction disjointness $W_2^o \cap (J \cup V) = W_2^i \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}.

``Head'' of a directed edge $(a, b) \in E$ means its second component $b$; ``endpoints'' of a bidirected edge $(a, b) \in L$ means the pair $\{a, b\}$ of its first and second components.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Node sets.}

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Input nodes ($J$).}
We show
\[ J_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \]
By def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~i.\ applied to $G$ with intervention set $W_1$,
\[ J_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; J \cup W_1. \]
By def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} item~i.\ applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with split set $W_2$ (well-typed by $W_2 \subseteq V \sm W_1 = V_{\doit(W_1)}$),
\[ J_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; J_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \dcup W_2^i \;=\; (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i. \]
The $\dcup$ here is a literal disjoint union: $W_2^i$ is disjoint from $J \cup W_1$ since $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ gives $(J \cup W_1) \subseteq J \cup V$, and $W_2^i \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$ by the tagged-construction disjointness from the notational reminder.

For the RHS, by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} item~i.\ applied to $G$ with split set $W_2$,
\[ J_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; J \dcup W_2^i. \]
By def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~i.\ applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ with intervention set $W_1$ (lifted via the unsplit injection per the well-typedness paragraph),
\[ J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; J_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \cup W_1 \;=\; (J \dcup W_2^i) \cup W_1. \]
By $W_1 \cap W_2^i = \emptyset$ (from the notational reminder), adjoining $W_1$ to $J \dcup W_2^i$ folds $W_1$ into the $J$ piece while leaving the $W_2^i$ piece untouched:
\[ (J \dcup W_2^i) \cup W_1 \;=\; (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i. \]
Hence
\[ J_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i \;=\; J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \]
\checkmark

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Output nodes ($V$).}
We show
\[ V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \]
By def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~ii.\ applied to $G$ with intervention set $W_1$ and then def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} item~ii.\ applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with split set $W_2 \subseteq V \sm W_1 = V_{\doit(W_1)}$,
\[
    V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; \lp V_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \sm W_2 \rp \;\dcup\; W_2^o
    \;=\; \lp (V \sm W_1) \sm W_2 \rp \;\dcup\; W_2^o,
\]
which simplifies (by $(V \sm W_1) \sm W_2 = V \sm W_1 \sm W_2$, the standard ``two-step removal'' identity for set-difference) to
\[
    V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o.
\]

For the RHS, by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} item~ii.\ applied to $G$ with split set $W_2$ and then def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~ii.\ applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ with intervention set $W_1$ (lifted to the SWIG carrier via the unsplit injection),
\[
    V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}
    \;=\; V_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \sm W_1
    \;=\; \lp (V \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o \rp \sm W_1.
\]
We simplify the latter by decomposing the set-difference across the two disjoint pieces of the disjoint union, using both the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ (on the untagged piece) and the SWIG-specific tagged-construction disjointness (on the tagged piece):
\begin{itemize}
    \item $W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset$: this is the SWIG-specific carrier-arithmetic observation from the notational reminder. Since the lifted $W_1$ sits in the untagged piece $J \cup (V \sm W_2) \subseteq J \cup V$, and $W_2^o \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$ by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s tagged-construction disjointness, the lifted $W_1$ does not meet $W_2^o$. Hence the set-difference acts as the identity on $W_2^o$.
    \item $(V \sm W_2) \sm W_1 = V \sm W_1 \sm W_2$: indeed, for any $x$, the chain of equivalences $x \in (V \sm W_2) \sm W_1$ iff $x \in V$, $x \notin W_2$, $x \notin W_1$ iff $x \in V$, $x \notin W_1$, $x \notin W_2$ iff $x \in V \sm W_1 \sm W_2$ holds. (Here the disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ is \emph{not} needed for this equality; the two iterated removals commute by associativity of $\sm$ alone.)
\end{itemize}
Combining the two piecewise calculations,
\[
    \lp (V \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o \rp \sm W_1
    \;=\; \lp (V \sm W_2) \sm W_1 \rp \;\dcup\; W_2^o
    \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o.
\]
Hence
\[
    V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o
    \;=\; V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}.
\]
\checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Directed edges.}
We show
\[ E_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \]
By def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~iii.\ applied to $G$ with intervention set $W_1$,
\[
    E_{G_{\doit(W_1)}}
    \;=\; E \sm \lC (v_1, v_2) \in E \st v_2 \in W_1 \rC
    \;=\; \lC (v_1, v_2) \in E \st v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]
By def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} item~iii.\ applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with split set $W_2$ (every surviving directed edge $(v_1, v_2) \in E_{G_{\doit(W_1)}}$ is lifted to $(v_1^i, v_2^o)$; note that the SWIG construction of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} introduces \emph{no} transfer edges, in contrast to the regular node-splitting $\spl$ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}),
\[
    E_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \rC
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]

For the RHS, by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} item~iii.\ applied to $G$ with split set $W_2$,
\[
    E_{G_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC.
\]
By def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~iii.\ applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ with intervention set $W_1$ (lifted to the SWIG carrier via the unsplit injection),
\[
    E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}
    \;=\; E_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \sm \lC (a, b) \in E_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \st b \in W_1 \rC.
\]
The removal condition ``$b \in W_1$'' inspects the head $b = v_2^o$ of each candidate generator $(v_1^i, v_2^o)$ arising from $(v_1, v_2) \in E$. By def \ref{def-cdmg}'s typing $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ we have $v_2 \in V$, so we case-split on whether $v_2 \in W_2$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{If $v_2 \in W_2$:} $v_2^o \in W_2^o$ is a tagged copy, lying in the disjoint-union piece $W_2^o$. By $W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset$ (from the notational reminder), $v_2^o \notin W_1$ (read in the SWIG carrier via the unsplit lift). The edge \emph{survives} the RHS removal. Cross-check with the LHS: by the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, $v_2 \in W_2$ also forces $v_2 \notin W_1$, so the LHS-side condition ``$v_2 \notin W_1$'' is automatically met -- both sides keep this edge.
    \item \emph{If $v_2 \in V \sm W_2$:} $v_2^o = v_2$ (unsplit shorthand, since $v_2 \in V \sm W_2 \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_2)$). The lifted $W_1$ in the SWIG carrier is the unsplit image $\lC w \st w \in W_1 \rC$, so $v_2^o \in W_1$ iff $v_2 \in W_1$ (the unsplit injection $J \cup (V \sm W_2) \hookrightarrow J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$ is injective). The edge is removed by the RHS iff $v_2 \in W_1$, equivalently kept iff $v_2 \notin W_1$. This matches the LHS condition ``$v_2 \notin W_1$'' verbatim.
\end{itemize}
Combining the two sub-cases, the generator $(v_1^i, v_2^o)$ from $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ survives the RHS removal iff $v_2 \notin W_1$, matching the LHS surviving condition exactly. (In the $v_2 \in W_2$ branch this is supplied automatically by the disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$; in the $v_2 \in V \sm W_2$ branch it is read off the unsplit identification.) Therefore
\[
    E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]
Comparing with the LHS,
\[
    E_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC
    \;=\; E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}.
\]
\checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Bidirected edges.}
We show
\[ L_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \]

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Registered two-sided removal of $L$.}
The Lean encoding of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~iv.\ filters $L_{\doit(W)}$ \emph{two-sidedly}: an ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ is removed whenever \emph{either} endpoint lies in $W$, not only when the second component does. This is the registered deviation \emph{hard\_intervention\_l\_symmetrized\_removal} attached to def\_3\_10's encoding, motivated in its remark on the symmetry of $L$: the LN's literal one-sided removal set $\lC (v_1, v_2) \in L \st v_2 \in W \rC$ does not in general preserve the symmetry axiom of def \ref{def-cdmg}, since for $w \in W \cap V$ and $v \in V \sm W$ both ordered pairs $(v, w)$ and $(w, v)$ lie in $L$ by symmetry but only $(v, w)$ is caught by the one-sided clause. The two-sided clause ``$v_1 \in W \vee v_2 \in W$'' symmetrises the removal and agrees with the LN's natural-language gloss ``remove all edges into nodes from $W$'' (a bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ is into \emph{both} of its endpoints). Crucially, under the symmetry axiom of $L$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}'s axiom $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$), the LN's one-sided form and the registered two-sided form produce the \emph{same} survivor set: a pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ survives the one-sided filter iff $v_2 \notin W$, and its symmetric partner $(v_2, v_1) \in L$ (which is also in $L$ by symmetry) survives iff $v_1 \notin W$; closing under symmetry, both readings land on
\[
    \lC (v_1, v_2) \in L \st v_1 \notin W,\; v_2 \notin W \rC.
\]
We adopt the two-sided form throughout the calculations below; the LN's literal one-sided gloss ``$L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$'' of \texttt{graphs.tex} lines 692--698 yields the same survivor set by the just-noted symmetric-closure argument.

By def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~iv.\ (two-sided), applied to $G$ with intervention set $W_1$,
\[
    L_{G_{\doit(W_1)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1, v_2) \in L \st v_1 \notin W_1,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]
By def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} item~iv.\ applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with split set $W_2$ (every surviving bidirected edge $(v_1, v_2) \in L_{G_{\doit(W_1)}}$ is lifted to $(v_1^o, v_2^o)$, with \emph{both} endpoints carrying the output-side $^o$ superscript -- in particular no element of $W_2^i$ appears as an endpoint of any bidirected edge),
\[
    L_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \rC
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L,\; v_1 \notin W_1,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]

For the RHS, by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} item~iv.\ applied to $G$ with split set $W_2$,
\[
    L_{G_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L \rC.
\]
By def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} item~iv.\ (two-sided), applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ with intervention set $W_1$ (lifted to the SWIG carrier via the unsplit injection),
\[
    L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}
    \;=\; \lC (a, b) \in L_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \st a \notin W_1,\; b \notin W_1 \rC.
\]
We compute the surviving pairs by analysing each endpoint of the generator $(v_1^o, v_2^o)$ arising from $(v_1, v_2) \in L$. By def \ref{def-cdmg}'s typing $L \subseteq V \times V$ we have $v_1, v_2 \in V$. For each $k \in \{1, 2\}$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{If $v_k \in W_2$:} $v_k^o \in W_2^o$ is a tagged copy. By $W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset$ (from the notational reminder), $v_k^o \notin W_1$ (in the SWIG carrier). Cross-check with the LHS: by the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, $v_k \in W_2$ also forces $v_k \notin W_1$, so both the RHS-side condition ``$v_k^o \notin W_1$'' and the LHS-side condition ``$v_k \notin W_1$'' are satisfied automatically in this branch.
    \item \emph{If $v_k \in V \sm W_2$:} $v_k^o = v_k$ (unsplit shorthand, since $v_k \in V \sm W_2 \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_2)$). The lifted $W_1$ in the SWIG carrier is the unsplit image $\lC w \st w \in W_1 \rC$, so $v_k^o \in W_1$ iff $v_k \in W_1$ (the unsplit injection is injective). Hence the RHS-side condition ``$v_k^o \notin W_1$'' is equivalent to the LHS-side condition ``$v_k \notin W_1$''.
\end{itemize}
In both sub-cases the condition ``$v_k^o \notin W_1$'' is equivalent to ``$v_k \notin W_1$''. Applying this to both endpoints $v_1$ and $v_2$ independently,
\[
    L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L,\; v_1 \notin W_1,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]
Comparing with the LHS,
\[
    L_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}
    \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L,\; v_1 \notin W_1,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC
    \;=\; L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}.
\]
\checkmark

\smallskip
Combining the four componentwise equalities ($J$, $V$, $E$, $L$) above, the CDMGs $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ and $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ agree field by field on each of $(J, V, E, L)$ and are therefore equal as CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}). The upgrade to an equality of CADMGs is automatic: acyclicity is a propositional condition on a fixed CDMG and transports along the CDMG-equality (acyclicity of the LHS follows from \ref{claim_3_9} (\emph{SwigAcyclic}) applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$, which is itself a CADMG by \ref{claim_3_3}; acyclicity of the RHS follows from \ref{claim_3_3} applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$, which is itself a CADMG by \ref{claim_3_9}).
\end{proof}

\end{document}

Lemma — Disjoint hard interventions and node-splitting hard interventions commute

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG (def def-cdmg-types, item i., equivalently a CDMG of def def-cdmg that is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). We rely throughout on the hard-intervention construction $\doit$ of def def:G_hard_intervention and the node-splitting hard-intervention (SWIG) construction $\swig$ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention. Let \[ W_1 \subseteq J \cup V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \subseteq V \] be two subsets of nodes of $G$ (both automatically finite, since $J \cup V$ is finite by def def-cdmg; this matches the project's Lean encoding of $W_1$ and $W_2$ as Finsets), subject to the disjointness side condition \[ W_1 \cap W_2 \;=\; \emptyset. \] All three quantifiers “for every CADMG $G$”, “for every $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$”, and “for every $W_2 \subseteq V$ with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$” are read universally; in particular the (vacuously disjoint) case $W_1 = W_2 = \emptyset$ is admitted.

Reading of “CADMG” in the LN's prose. The LN's literal text opens with “Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG” and refers in the prose to “the CADMG obtained from $\ldots$” for both sides of the displayed equality. Both sides of the equality are produced by applying $\doit$ (def def:G_hard_intervention) and $\swig$ (def def:G_node-splitting_intervention) to $G$. The constructor of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention requires the CADMG hypothesis on its input – on $G$ for the RHS's inner $\swig(W_2)$, and on $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ for the LHS's outer $\swig(W_2)$. Acyclicity of $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ under the CADMG hypothesis on $G$ is established by claim_3_3 (AcyclicPreservedUnderDo); acyclicity of $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ under the same hypothesis is established by claim_3_9 (SwigAcyclic). Hence both sides of the displayed equality are CADMGs. At the underlying CDMG-type level, the asserted equality below is first an equality of CDMGs (def def-cdmg) – componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ – and the upgrade to an equality of CADMGs is automatic once both sides are known to be acyclic CDMGs (acyclicity is a propositional condition on a fixed CDMG, transported along the CDMG-equality).

Well-typedness of the inner SWIG on the LHS. The LHS $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ requires its outer $\swig(W_2)$ to act on a CADMG whose output-node set contains $W_2$, i.e.\ $W_2 \subseteq V_{\doit(W_1)}$ per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's precondition (the CADMG-ness of $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ itself comes from claim_3_3 on top of $G$ being a CADMG). By item ii.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention we have $V_{\doit(W_1)} = V \sm W_1$, and the inclusion \[ W_2 \;\subseteq\; V \sm W_1 \;=\; V_{\doit(W_1)} \] follows from $W_2 \subseteq V$ together with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$. Hence the LHS is defined per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention. Well-typedness of the RHS $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ is spelled out in the carrier-reading paragraph below the displayed equality.

The conclusion. The CADMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting (in the SWIG sense of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention) on $W_2$ equals the CADMG obtained from first node-splitting (in the same sense) on $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$: \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}. \] The equality is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg: equal input-node sets ($J_{\text{LHS}} = J_{\text{RHS}}$), equal output-node sets ($V_{\text{LHS}} = V_{\text{RHS}}$), equal directed-edge sets ($E_{\text{LHS}} = E_{\text{RHS}}$), and equal bidirected-edge sets ($L_{\text{LHS}} = L_{\text{RHS}}$); the conjunctive unpacking into these four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

Carrier reading of the equality. By def def:G_node-splitting_intervention the operation $\swig(W_2)$ shifts the carrier from $J \cup V$ to the tagged-disjoint-union carrier \[ J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp J \dcup W_2^i \rp \;\cup\; \lp (V \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o \rp, \] where $W_2^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W_2 \rC$ and $W_2^i := \lC w^i \st w \in W_2 \rC$ are the two type-level tagged copies of $W_2$ introduced in def def:G_node-splitting_intervention (so $W_2^o \cap W_2^i = \emptyset$ and both $W_2^o$ and $W_2^i$ are disjoint from every element of $J \cup V$). Crucially, the input-side tagged copy $W_2^i$ sits in $J_{\swig(W_2)}$ and the output-side tagged copy $W_2^o$ sits in $V_{\swig(W_2)}$ – this is the distinguishing feature of $\swig$ versus the regular node-splitting $\spl$ of def def:G_node-splitting, whose output-node set carries both tagged copies. By def def:G_hard_intervention the operation $\doit$ preserves the carrier $J \cup V$ of its input as a set (items i.\ and ii.\ give $J_{\doit(W)} \cup V_{\doit(W)} = (J \cup W) \cup (V \sm W) = J \cup V$ whenever $W \subseteq J \cup V$). Thus both sides of the displayed equality live in a CDMG over the same $\swig(W_2)$-carrier just displayed, and the asserted equality is a literal $=$ of CDMGs (def def-cdmg) – not an equality up to a carrier-relabelling map. On the right-hand side, $\doit(W_1)$ is read as hard-intervening on the unsplit copies of the nodes of $W_1$ inside the $\swig(W_2)$-carrier: every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in J \cup V$ (by $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$) and $w \notin W_2$ (by $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$), so $w \in J \cup (V \sm W_2)$, which is the untagged piece of $J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$ under def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's notational shorthand “$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_2)$” (i.e.\ $W_1$ injects into the $\swig(W_2)$-carrier as the unsplit injection $w \mapsto w$). The precondition $W_1 \subseteq J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$ of def def:G_hard_intervention is thereby met by these unsplit copies, so the RHS is also defined per def def:G_hard_intervention, and the LN's $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ carrier reading agrees with the unique LN-faithful reading of $\doit(W_1)$ on it.

Proof.

We verify the equality $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ componentwise on the four fields $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg, organised as in the LN under three section headings: “Node sets” (covering $J$ and $V$ jointly), “Directed edges” (the $E$ field), and “Bidirected edges” (the $L$ field).

Well-typedness of both iteration orders. For the LHS $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$: the outer $\swig(W_2)$ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention requires (a) its input to be a CADMG and (b) $W_2 \subseteq V_{\doit(W_1)}$. For (a), $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ is itself a CADMG by claim_3_3 (AcyclicPreservedUnderDo) applied to the standing CADMG hypothesis on $G$ – this is the SWIG-specific acyclicity step that the spl analog (claim_3_8) did not need, since the regular node-splitting $\spl$ of def def:G_node-splitting does not require a CADMG input. For (b), by def def:G_hard_intervention item ii., $V_{\doit(W_1)} = V \sm W_1$, and the inclusion $W_2 \subseteq V \sm W_1$ follows from $W_2 \subseteq V$ together with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$. For the RHS $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$: the outer $\doit(W_1)$ of def def:G_hard_intervention requires only $W_1 \subseteq J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$, which holds by the carrier-reading paragraph of the statement above: every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in J \cup V$ (from $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$) and $w \notin W_2$ (from $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$), so $w \in J \cup (V \sm W_2)$, the untagged piece of $J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$ under def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's unsplit shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$.

Notational reminder. For $w \in W_2$ we write $w^o$, $w^i$ for the two tagged copies produced by $\swig(W_2)$ per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention; these live in the tagged-disjoint-union pieces $W_2^o$ and $W_2^i$ respectively, and are formally distinct from every element of $J \cup V$ (and from each other), per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's tagged-copies disjointness assertions $W_2^o \cap V = W_2^i \cap V = W_2^o \cap J = W_2^i \cap J = W_2^o \cap W_2^i = \emptyset$. For $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_2)$ we use def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's unsplit shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$, which leaves $v$ in the untagged piece of $J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$. Crucially for SWIG, the input copies $W_2^i$ sit in $J_{\swig(W_2)}$ and the output copies $W_2^o$ sit in $V_{\swig(W_2)}$ – the distinguishing feature of $\swig$ versus the regular node-splitting $\spl$ of def def:G_node-splitting, whose output-node set carries both tagged copies.

On the right-hand side, the outer $\doit(W_1)$ is read on the lifted intervention set “$W_1$ inside the $\swig(W_2)$-carrier”, which by the carrier-reading paragraph of the statement is the unsplit image $\lC w \st w \in W_1 \rC \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_2)$ (using $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ and $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$); throughout the proof we write $W_1$ for this lifted set, using the unsplit shorthand $w = w^o = w^i$ for $w \in W_1$. The SWIG-specific carrier-arithmetic observation we use repeatedly below is \[ W_1 \cap W_2^o \;=\; \emptyset \qquad\text{and}\qquad W_1 \cap W_2^i \;=\; \emptyset, \] both of which follow from $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ together with the tagged-construction disjointness $W_2^o \cap (J \cup V) = W_2^i \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention.

“Head” of a directed edge $(a, b) \in E$ means its second component $b$; “endpoints” of a bidirected edge $(a, b) \in L$ means the pair $\{a, b\}$ of its first and second components.

Node sets.

Input nodes ($J$). We show \[ J_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \] By def def:G_hard_intervention item i.\ applied to $G$ with intervention set $W_1$, \[ J_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; J \cup W_1. \] By def def:G_node-splitting_intervention item i.\ applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with split set $W_2$ (well-typed by $W_2 \subseteq V \sm W_1 = V_{\doit(W_1)}$), \[ J_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; J_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \dcup W_2^i \;=\; (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i. \] The $\dcup$ here is a literal disjoint union: $W_2^i$ is disjoint from $J \cup W_1$ since $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ gives $(J \cup W_1) \subseteq J \cup V$, and $W_2^i \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$ by the tagged-construction disjointness from the notational reminder.

For the RHS, by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention item i.\ applied to $G$ with split set $W_2$, \[ J_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; J \dcup W_2^i. \] By def def:G_hard_intervention item i.\ applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ with intervention set $W_1$ (lifted via the unsplit injection per the well-typedness paragraph), \[ J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; J_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \cup W_1 \;=\; (J \dcup W_2^i) \cup W_1. \] By $W_1 \cap W_2^i = \emptyset$ (from the notational reminder), adjoining $W_1$ to $J \dcup W_2^i$ folds $W_1$ into the $J$ piece while leaving the $W_2^i$ piece untouched: \[ (J \dcup W_2^i) \cup W_1 \;=\; (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i. \] Hence \[ J_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i \;=\; J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \] ✓

Output nodes ($V$). We show \[ V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \] By def def:G_hard_intervention item ii.\ applied to $G$ with intervention set $W_1$ and then def def:G_node-splitting_intervention item ii.\ applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with split set $W_2 \subseteq V \sm W_1 = V_{\doit(W_1)}$, \[ V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lp V_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \sm W_2 \rp \;\dcup\; W_2^o \;=\; \lp (V \sm W_1) \sm W_2 \rp \;\dcup\; W_2^o, \] which simplifies (by $(V \sm W_1) \sm W_2 = V \sm W_1 \sm W_2$, the standard “two-step removal” identity for set-difference) to \[ V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o. \]

For the RHS, by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention item ii.\ applied to $G$ with split set $W_2$ and then def def:G_hard_intervention item ii.\ applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ with intervention set $W_1$ (lifted to the SWIG carrier via the unsplit injection), \[ V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; V_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \sm W_1 \;=\; \lp (V \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o \rp \sm W_1. \] We simplify the latter by decomposing the set-difference across the two disjoint pieces of the disjoint union, using both the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ (on the untagged piece) and the SWIG-specific tagged-construction disjointness (on the tagged piece):

  • $W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset$: this is the SWIG-specific carrier-arithmetic observation from the notational reminder. Since the lifted $W_1$ sits in the untagged piece $J \cup (V \sm W_2) \subseteq J \cup V$, and $W_2^o \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$ by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's tagged-construction disjointness, the lifted $W_1$ does not meet $W_2^o$. Hence the set-difference acts as the identity on $W_2^o$.
  • $(V \sm W_2) \sm W_1 = V \sm W_1 \sm W_2$: indeed, for any $x$, the chain of equivalences $x \in (V \sm W_2) \sm W_1$ iff $x \in V$, $x \notin W_2$, $x \notin W_1$ iff $x \in V$, $x \notin W_1$, $x \notin W_2$ iff $x \in V \sm W_1 \sm W_2$ holds. (Here the disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ is not needed for this equality; the two iterated removals commute by associativity of $\sm$ alone.)

Combining the two piecewise calculations, \[ \lp (V \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o \rp \sm W_1 \;=\; \lp (V \sm W_2) \sm W_1 \rp \;\dcup\; W_2^o \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o. \] Hence \[ V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_2^o \;=\; V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \] ✓

Directed edges. We show \[ E_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \] By def def:G_hard_intervention item iii.\ applied to $G$ with intervention set $W_1$, \[ E_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; E \sm \lC (v_1, v_2) \in E \st v_2 \in W_1 \rC \;=\; \lC (v_1, v_2) \in E \st v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \] By def def:G_node-splitting_intervention item iii.\ applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with split set $W_2$ (every surviving directed edge $(v_1, v_2) \in E_{G_{\doit(W_1)}}$ is lifted to $(v_1^i, v_2^o)$; note that the SWIG construction of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention introduces no transfer edges, in contrast to the regular node-splitting $\spl$ of def def:G_node-splitting), \[ E_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \rC \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \]

For the RHS, by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention item iii.\ applied to $G$ with split set $W_2$, \[ E_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC. \] By def def:G_hard_intervention item iii.\ applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ with intervention set $W_1$ (lifted to the SWIG carrier via the unsplit injection), \[ E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; E_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \sm \lC (a, b) \in E_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \st b \in W_1 \rC. \] The removal condition “$b \in W_1$” inspects the head $b = v_2^o$ of each candidate generator $(v_1^i, v_2^o)$ arising from $(v_1, v_2) \in E$. By def def-cdmg's typing $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ we have $v_2 \in V$, so we case-split on whether $v_2 \in W_2$:

  • If $v_2 \in W_2$: $v_2^o \in W_2^o$ is a tagged copy, lying in the disjoint-union piece $W_2^o$. By $W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset$ (from the notational reminder), $v_2^o \notin W_1$ (read in the SWIG carrier via the unsplit lift). The edge survives the RHS removal. Cross-check with the LHS: by the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, $v_2 \in W_2$ also forces $v_2 \notin W_1$, so the LHS-side condition “$v_2 \notin W_1$” is automatically met – both sides keep this edge.
  • If $v_2 \in V \sm W_2$: $v_2^o = v_2$ (unsplit shorthand, since $v_2 \in V \sm W_2 \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_2)$). The lifted $W_1$ in the SWIG carrier is the unsplit image $\lC w \st w \in W_1 \rC$, so $v_2^o \in W_1$ iff $v_2 \in W_1$ (the unsplit injection $J \cup (V \sm W_2) \hookrightarrow J_{\swig(W_2)} \cup V_{\swig(W_2)}$ is injective). The edge is removed by the RHS iff $v_2 \in W_1$, equivalently kept iff $v_2 \notin W_1$. This matches the LHS condition “$v_2 \notin W_1$” verbatim.

Combining the two sub-cases, the generator $(v_1^i, v_2^o)$ from $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ survives the RHS removal iff $v_2 \notin W_1$, matching the LHS surviving condition exactly. (In the $v_2 \in W_2$ branch this is supplied automatically by the disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$; in the $v_2 \in V \sm W_2$ branch it is read off the unsplit identification.) Therefore \[ E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \] Comparing with the LHS, \[ E_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC \;=\; E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \] ✓

Bidirected edges. We show \[ L_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \]

Registered two-sided removal of $L$. The Lean encoding of def def:G_hard_intervention item iv.\ filters $L_{\doit(W)}$ two-sidedly: an ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ is removed whenever either endpoint lies in $W$, not only when the second component does. This is the registered deviation hard\_intervention\_l\_symmetrized\_removal attached to def\_3\_10's encoding, motivated in its remark on the symmetry of $L$: the LN's literal one-sided removal set $\lC (v_1, v_2) \in L \st v_2 \in W \rC$ does not in general preserve the symmetry axiom of def def-cdmg, since for $w \in W \cap V$ and $v \in V \sm W$ both ordered pairs $(v, w)$ and $(w, v)$ lie in $L$ by symmetry but only $(v, w)$ is caught by the one-sided clause. The two-sided clause “$v_1 \in W \vee v_2 \in W$” symmetrises the removal and agrees with the LN's natural-language gloss “remove all edges into nodes from $W$” (a bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ is into both of its endpoints). Crucially, under the symmetry axiom of $L$ (def def-cdmg's axiom $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$), the LN's one-sided form and the registered two-sided form produce the same survivor set: a pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ survives the one-sided filter iff $v_2 \notin W$, and its symmetric partner $(v_2, v_1) \in L$ (which is also in $L$ by symmetry) survives iff $v_1 \notin W$; closing under symmetry, both readings land on \[ \lC (v_1, v_2) \in L \st v_1 \notin W,\; v_2 \notin W \rC. \] We adopt the two-sided form throughout the calculations below; the LN's literal one-sided gloss “$L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$” of graphs.tex lines 692–698 yields the same survivor set by the just-noted symmetric-closure argument.

By def def:G_hard_intervention item iv.\ (two-sided), applied to $G$ with intervention set $W_1$, \[ L_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1, v_2) \in L \st v_1 \notin W_1,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \] By def def:G_node-splitting_intervention item iv.\ applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with split set $W_2$ (every surviving bidirected edge $(v_1, v_2) \in L_{G_{\doit(W_1)}}$ is lifted to $(v_1^o, v_2^o)$, with both endpoints carrying the output-side $^o$ superscript – in particular no element of $W_2^i$ appears as an endpoint of any bidirected edge), \[ L_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L_{G_{\doit(W_1)}} \rC \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L,\; v_1 \notin W_1,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \]

For the RHS, by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention item iv.\ applied to $G$ with split set $W_2$, \[ L_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L \rC. \] By def def:G_hard_intervention item iv.\ (two-sided), applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ with intervention set $W_1$ (lifted to the SWIG carrier via the unsplit injection), \[ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC (a, b) \in L_{G_{\swig(W_2)}} \st a \notin W_1,\; b \notin W_1 \rC. \] We compute the surviving pairs by analysing each endpoint of the generator $(v_1^o, v_2^o)$ arising from $(v_1, v_2) \in L$. By def def-cdmg's typing $L \subseteq V \times V$ we have $v_1, v_2 \in V$. For each $k \in \{1, 2\}$:

  • If $v_k \in W_2$: $v_k^o \in W_2^o$ is a tagged copy. By $W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset$ (from the notational reminder), $v_k^o \notin W_1$ (in the SWIG carrier). Cross-check with the LHS: by the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, $v_k \in W_2$ also forces $v_k \notin W_1$, so both the RHS-side condition “$v_k^o \notin W_1$” and the LHS-side condition “$v_k \notin W_1$” are satisfied automatically in this branch.
  • If $v_k \in V \sm W_2$: $v_k^o = v_k$ (unsplit shorthand, since $v_k \in V \sm W_2 \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_2)$). The lifted $W_1$ in the SWIG carrier is the unsplit image $\lC w \st w \in W_1 \rC$, so $v_k^o \in W_1$ iff $v_k \in W_1$ (the unsplit injection is injective). Hence the RHS-side condition “$v_k^o \notin W_1$” is equivalent to the LHS-side condition “$v_k \notin W_1$”.

In both sub-cases the condition “$v_k^o \notin W_1$” is equivalent to “$v_k \notin W_1$”. Applying this to both endpoints $v_1$ and $v_2$ independently, \[ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L,\; v_1 \notin W_1,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \] Comparing with the LHS, \[ L_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L,\; v_1 \notin W_1,\; v_2 \notin W_1 \rC \;=\; L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}}. \] ✓

Combining the four componentwise equalities ($J$, $V$, $E$, $L$) above, the CDMGs $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ and $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ agree field by field on each of $(J, V, E, L)$ and are therefore equal as CDMGs (def def-cdmg). The upgrade to an equality of CADMGs is automatic: acyclicity is a propositional condition on a fixed CDMG and transports along the CDMG-equality (acyclicity of the LHS follows from claim_3_9 (SwigAcyclic) applied to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$, which is itself a CADMG by claim_3_3; acyclicity of the RHS follows from claim_3_3 applied to $G_{\swig(W_2)}$, which is itself a CADMG by claim_3_9).

The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for this row in particular: the wrapped signature directly mentions `Disjoint W₁ W₂` on `Finset Node` and `W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit`, and traverses `G.J`, `G.V`, `G.E`, `G.L` through `G.IsCADMG`, `G.hardInterventionOn`, and `G.nodeSplittingHard` — each consumes `[DecidableEq Node]` through `Finset`-backed membership, image, `Finset.filter`, `Finset.union`, and `Finset.sdiff` operations. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.

    {W₂ : Finset Node} (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :

The conclusion `W₂ ⊆ (G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).V` is exactly the precondition demanded by the LHS's outer `nodeSplittingHard` — namely that the SWIG operation can split on $W_2$ inside the post-intervention graph. By `def_3_10` item ii., `(G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).V` definitionally reduces to `G.V \ W₁`, so the goal is $W_2 \subseteq V \setminus W_1$; this follows from `hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V` together with disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$. The hypothesis is bundled as Mathlib's lattice predicate `Disjoint W₁ W₂` (the canonical `Finset` form used by `claim_3_8` and `claim_3_10`), consumed in one step by `Finset.disjoint_right`. The lemma is wrapped with helper markers and travels with the rendered statement on the website, because the main signature directly references its name in the LHS's outer SWIG precondition slot — removing it would break type-checking of the wrapped theorem head.

private lemma image_unsplit_subset_carrier_of_nodeSplittingHard
    {G : CDMG Node} {hG : G.IsCADMG}
    {W₁ : Finset Node} (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    {W₂ : Finset Node} (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit ⊆
      (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₂ hW₂).J ∪ (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₂ hW₂).V
lean_blocks.explanation: This `private lemma` discharges the second well-typedness side condition of the main theorem: it proves that the unsplit-lifted $W_1$ sits inside the node carrier of the SWIG-split graph $G_{\mathrm{swig}(W_2)}$. The conclusion `W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit ⊆ (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₂ hW₂).J ∪ (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₂ hW₂).V` is exactly the precondition demanded by the RHS's outer `hardInterventionOn`. The encoding faithfully renders the LN's carrier-reading paragraph: every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in J \cup V$ (by `hW₁`) and $w \notin W_2$ (by disjointness), so $w$ injects into the split-graph carrier as its unsplit copy via `Finset.image SplitNode.unsplit`. The proof case-splits `w ∈ G.J` versus `w ∈ G.V`: in the former branch `.unsplit w` lands in the left summand of $J_{\mathrm{swig}(W_2)}$; in the latter, disjointness gives `w ∉ W₂`, placing `.unsplit w` in the `(G.V \ W₂).image .unsplit` piece of $V_{\mathrm{swig}(W_2)}$. The `{hG : G.IsCADMG}` binder is propagated as an implicit because `nodeSplittingHard` (def 3.12) requires acyclicity on its input.

-- Private helper: discharges the RHS's outer hard-intervention precondition for the unsplit-lifted `W₁`.
private lemma image_unsplit_subset_carrier_of_nodeSplittingHard
    -- CADMG witness `hG` propagated as implicit; required by `nodeSplittingHard` (def 3.12).
    {G : CDMG Node} {hG : G.IsCADMG}
    -- `W₁ ⊆ J ∪ V` matches the LN's hard-intervention target typing.
    {W₁ : Finset Node} (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    -- `W₂ ⊆ V` is the SWIG target; `Disjoint W₁ W₂` lifts `W₁` cleanly into the untagged piece.
    {W₂ : Finset Node} (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    -- Lift `W₁` to the split carrier via `Finset.image SplitNode.unsplit` (LN's `v^o := v^i := v` shorthand).
    W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit ⊆
      -- Conclusion: lifted `W₁` lands inside the SWIG-split carrier `J_{swig(W₂)} ∪ V_{swig(W₂)}`.

lean_blocks.code:
theorem disjointHardInterventionsAndNodeSplittingHardsCommute
    (G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsCADMG)
    (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V)
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).nodeSplittingHard
        (hardInterventionOn_isCADMG_of_isCADMG hG hW₁) W₂
        (subset_V_of_hardInterventionOn hW₁ hW₂ hDisj)
      = (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₂ hW₂).hardInterventionOn
          (W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit)
          (image_unsplit_subset_carrier_of_nodeSplittingHard hW₁ hW₂ hDisj)
lean_blocks.explanation: This is the main `theorem` of the row, asserting that the two iterated constructions commute: applying hard intervention on $W_1$ followed by SWIG node-splitting on $W_2$ yields exactly the same `CDMG (SplitNode Node)` as applying SWIG on $W_2$ followed by hard intervention on the unsplit lift of $W_1$. Because `hardInterventionOn` (def 3.10) preserves the node carrier and `nodeSplittingHard` (def 3.12) lifts it to `SplitNode Node`, both sides land in the identical Lean type, so the equality is a literal structural `=` of CDMGs — no `eqViaNodeMap` or `flattenSplit` workaround is required (contrast `claim_3_10`). The `(hG : G.IsCADMG)` binder is mandatory: `nodeSplittingHard` requires acyclicity on its input, both for the RHS's inner SWIG (fed `hG` directly) and for the LHS's outer SWIG (whose input is acyclic via the private wrapper `hardInterventionOn_isCADMG_of_isCADMG`, routed through `claim_3_3`). The two helper lemmas above the theorem supply the SWIG and hard-intervention preconditions in line, and `W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit` renders the LN's carrier-reading shorthand for $W_1$ inside the split-graph carrier.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Main theorem of `claim_3_11`: literal `=` of CDMGs over `SplitNode Node`.
theorem disjointHardInterventionsAndNodeSplittingHardsCommute
    -- Ambient CADMG: data `G` plus the propositional acyclicity witness `hG`.
    (G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsCADMG)
    -- Hard-intervention target `W₁` and SWIG target `W₂`, both as `Finset Node`.
    (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V)
    -- Disjointness `W₁ ∩ W₂ = ∅` in Mathlib's lattice `Disjoint` form.
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    -- LHS: first `doit(W₁)`, then `swig(W₂)`; inner intervention keeps carrier as `Node`.
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).nodeSplittingHard
        -- Outer SWIG needs an `IsCADMG` witness on the intervened graph; supplied via `claim_3_3` wrapper.
        (hardInterventionOn_isCADMG_of_isCADMG hG hW₁) W₂
        -- Outer SWIG also needs `W₂ ⊆ (G_{doit(W₁)}).V`; discharged by the first helper above.
        (subset_V_of_hardInterventionOn hW₁ hW₂ hDisj)
      -- RHS: first `swig(W₂)` (lifts carrier to `SplitNode Node`), then `doit` on the unsplit lift of `W₁`.
      = (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₂ hW₂).hardInterventionOn
          -- Lift `W₁` to the split-graph carrier via `Finset.image SplitNode.unsplit`.
          (W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit)
          -- Outer hard intervention's carrier-precondition discharged by the second helper above.
          (image_unsplit_subset_carrier_of_nodeSplittingHard hW₁ hW₂ hDisj)

design_choices:
**Literal `=` of CDMGs over `SplitNode Node`, not `eqViaNodeMap` / `flattenSplit`.** This is the dominant structural simplification of this row relative to `claim_3_10` (`TwoDisjointNodeSwig`). Both sides apply a *single* SWIG node-splitting on $W_2$ and a single hard intervention on $W_1$, and `hardInterventionOn` preserves the node carrier (`CDMG α → CDMG α` per def 3.10), so both sides land in the identical Lean type `CDMG (SplitNode Node)`. Contrast `claim_3_10`, where iterating `nodeSplittingHard` twice produces `CDMG (SplitNode (SplitNode Node))` on both sides, with a node $w$ wearing disagreeing constructor wrappings (`.unsplit (.copy0 w)` vs `.copy0 (.unsplit w)`) and forcing the `eqViaNodeMap` / `flattenSplit` workaround. Here the LN's 'the same CADMG' reading is delivered by Lean's structural `=` directly; no carrier-relabelling map, no chase through a flatten function. Mirrors `claim_3_8`'s shape exactly, lifted to the SWIG side.

**`(hG : G.IsCADMG)` on the signature.** Required by def 3.12's `nodeSplittingHard`, both for the RHS's inner SWIG (fed `hG` directly) and for the LHS's outer SWIG (whose input `G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁` is acyclic by `claim_3_3`, routed through a one-line private wrapper that extracts the relevant conjunct). The LN's 'Let $G$ be a CADMG' opener maps exactly to this binder. `claim_3_8`'s sibling theorem has no such binder because def 3.11's regular `nodeSplittingOn` takes no acyclicity precondition — the key signature divergence between the two rows.

**Disjointness `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is genuinely load-bearing.** Two distinct uses appear inside the signature: the LHS's inner SWIG needs $W_2 \subseteq V \setminus W_1$ (discharged by `subset_V_of_hardInterventionOn`), and the RHS's outer hard intervention needs the unsplit-lifted $W_1$ to sit inside $J_{\mathrm{swig}(W_2)} \cup V_{\mathrm{swig}(W_2)}$ (discharged by `image_unsplit_subset_carrier_of_nodeSplittingHard`). Without disjointness, a node $w \in W_1 \cap W_2$ would simultaneously be hard-intervened (becomes an input on one side) and SWIG-split (gets new copies $w^o$, $w^i$ on the other), committing both sides to incompatible carrier placements; the equality would no longer hold. Encoded as Mathlib's `Disjoint`, matching `claim_3_8` and `claim_3_10`.

**Lift $W_1$ to the split carrier via `Finset.image SplitNode.unsplit`.** Every $w \in W_1$ lies in $J \cup (V \setminus W_2)$ by `hW₁` and disjointness — the untagged piece of the split-graph carrier under def 3.12's shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$. Lifting via `.image .unsplit` lets the RHS's outer hard intervention be expressed in terms of the *original* `W₁` rather than a fresh `Finset (SplitNode Node)`. Reuses the same convention as `claim_3_8` and `claim_3_10`.

**Single theorem, not a conjunction.** The LN statement is a single equality $\mathrm{LHS} = \mathrm{RHS}$, mirroring `claim_3_8`. No joint-intervention form exists because $\mathrm{doit}$ and $\mathrm{swig}$ are structurally distinct operations and do not admit a single combined invocation.

**Two-sided filter on $L$ (registered deviation).** The encoding of $\mathrm{doit}(W)$ in def 3.10 filters $L_{\mathrm{doit}(W)}$ two-sidedly — removing pairs whenever *either* endpoint lies in $W$ — instead of the LN's literal one-sided clause. The one-sided form does not preserve $L$'s symmetry axiom: for $w \in W$ and $v \notin W$ the symmetric partners $(v, w)$ and $(w, v)$ would otherwise be treated asymmetrically. Under the symmetry axiom both readings collapse to the same survivor set, so the bidirected-edge component is unaffected by the choice.
